feed.title: The Shorter Lab
feed.subtitle: The Shorter lab focuses on understanding the genetic mechanisms of disease, and the development and use of polygenic risk scores for disease prediction. .

entry.title: New paper: perampanel pharmacokinetics in children with epilepsy

link.title: New paper: perampanel pharmacokinetics in children with epilepsy

entry.content: Congratulations to Sara Angelucci, who led our study on the pharmacokinetics of perampanel in Danish paediatric patients with epilepsy, based on therapeutic drug monitoring. The work is now published in Basic & Clinical Pharmacology & Toxicology. Read the paper.
entry.summary: Congratulations to Sara Angelucci, who led our study on the pharmacokinetics of perampanel in Danish paediatric patients with epilepsy, based on therapeutic drug monitoring. The work is now published in Basic & Clinical Pharmacology & Toxicology. Read the paper.

entry.title: New paper: distinct genetic architectures for early- and late-onset depression

link.title: New paper: distinct genetic architectures for early- and late-onset depression

entry.content: Our genome-wide association analyses identifying distinct genetic architectures for early-onset and late-onset depression are out in Nature Genetics. The study points to partly different biology underlying depression depending on age of onset. Read the paper.
entry.summary: Our genome-wide association analyses identifying distinct genetic architectures for early-onset and late-onset depression are out in Nature Genetics. The study points to partly different biology underlying depression depending on age of onset. Read the paper.

entry.title: Understanding Population-Based Risk of Psychiatric Disorders Associated With Recurrent Copy Number Variants

link.title: Understanding Population-Based Risk of Psychiatric Disorders Associated With Recurrent Copy Number Variants

entry.content: This study provides population-based estimates for the prevalence and psychiatric disorder risk associated with recurrent copy number variants (rCNVs) by analyzing the Danish Integrative Psychiatric Research (iPSYCH) case-cohort. The overall take away was that many rCNVs are more common but carry less risk for conditions like ADHD, ASD, and SSD than previously indicated by case-control studies, suggesting overestimation of risk due to selection bias. Notably, no rCNV showed increased risk for major depressive disorder (MDD), and the risk associated with rCNVs correlated with the size and gene constraint of the affected genomic region, rather than the type of copy number change (deletion or duplication). To conclude, this study found that several rCNVs were more prevalent and conferred less risk of psychiatric disorders than estimated previously.
entry.summary: This study provides population-based estimates for the prevalence and psychiatric disorder risk associated with recurrent copy number variants (rCNVs) by analyzing the Danish Integrative Psychiatric Research (iPSYCH) case-cohort. The overall take away was that many rCNVs are more common but carry less risk for conditions like ADHD, ASD, and SSD than previously indicated by case-control studies, suggesting overestimation of risk due to selection bias. Notably, no rCNV showed increased risk for major depressive disorder (MDD), and the risk associated with rCNVs correlated with the size and gene constraint of the affected genomic region, rather than the type of copy number change (deletion or duplication). To conclude, this study found that several rCNVs were more prevalent and conferred less risk of psychiatric disorders than estimated previously.
